objectives.text: Distinguish gross pay from net pay
Identify which deductions are mandatory vs optional
Explain why a raise into a higher bracket never reduces take-home pay
Calculate net pay from a gross figure and deduction rates
Budget from take-home rather than gross salary

vocabulary.definition: The full amount earned before any taxes or deductions.
What actually hits your bank account — gross minus every deduction.
A mandatory 7.65% payroll deduction: 6.2% Social Security + 1.45% Medicare.
A tax system where higher earners pay a higher percentage on the income in each bracket.
The tax rate that applies only to the next dollar earned — not to your whole salary.

misconceptions.myth: A raise that pushes me into a higher tax bracket means I'll take home less money.
misconceptions.reality: Only the dollars above the bracket threshold are taxed at the higher rate. A raise always increases take-home pay — never decreases it.

misconceptions.myth: My $52,000 salary means I can spend $4,333 a month.
misconceptions.reality: After taxes and FICA, take-home is closer to $3,200/month. Budgeting from gross is one of the most common — and costliest — first-paycheck mistakes.

sections.content:
## A text from Jordan

> I just got my first paycheck!!
>
> Something's wrong — it says **$1,538** but my salary is **$52,000**.
>
> Can you help me figure out what happened?

Jordan just started her first full-time job. We're going to help her figure out where the missing money went — by building her paystub line by line.

sections.question: Before we dig in — where do you think the missing $462 went? (Quick gut check, no wrong answer.)
options: An error she needs to fix
sections.explanation: Your instinct was probably taxes — and that's most of it. But the full answer is more interesting: it's actually three different taxes, plus FICA, plus possibly a benefit she signed up for. Let's build the paystub together.

sections.question: Looking at Jordan's paystub: which number is her gross pay?
options: $2,000 — the total before anything is removed
sections.explanation: Gross is the top line — before deductions. Net is what hits the bank.

sections.setup:
**Unrelated scenario — new person.**

Your friend Maya just got a $45,000 offer in California and signed a lease for **$1,600/month**. She's pumped.
sections.question: Is Maya okay?
options: She's fine — $45K/12 = $3,750/month, so rent is under half
She's in trouble — her take-home is closer to $2,700/month, so $1,600 eats almost 60% of it
She's fine as long as she doesn't have a car payment
Impossible to say without knowing her credit score
sections.explanation: Maya budgeted from gross ($3,750/month). But take-home on $45K is closer to $2,700. That $1,050/month gap is the exact mistake this lesson is about.

sections.prompt: In one sentence, how would you explain the gap between Jordan's $52,000 salary and her $1,538 paycheck?

exit_ticket.question: You earn $1,000 gross. Federal tax takes 10%, state takes 4%, FICA takes 7.65%. What's your net pay?
A coworker turned down a bonus because 'it would push me into a higher tax bracket and I'd lose money.' What's wrong with that reasoning?
options: Only the dollars above the bracket threshold get taxed higher; the rest stays at the lower rate, so a bonus always increases take-home
Bonuses are taxed at a special 0% rate
The coworker is right only if they earn over $100K

exit_ticket.question: You get a $48,000 job offer and expect to keep about 72% after deductions. Roughly how much should you budget for each month?
